properties.name: 1978 Miyagi-Oki Earthquake
properties.description: A major offshore earthquake struck the Miyagi coast on June 12, 1978. It caused significant damage in Sendai and triggered a small tsunami.

properties.name: 1978 Izu-Oshima Earthquake
properties.description: A strong earthquake struck the Izu Peninsula on January 14, 1978. It caused landslides and structural damage in Shizuoka Prefecture.

properties.name: 1982 Urakawa-Oki Earthquake
properties.description: A magnitude 7.1 earthquake struck offshore Hokkaido on March 21, 1982. It was widely felt but caused limited damage due to its offshore location.

properties.name: 1983 Sea of Japan Earthquake
properties.description: The Nihonkai-Chubu earthquake struck offshore Akita on May 26, 1983. It generated a destructive tsunami that killed 100 people and caused widespread coastal damage.

properties.name: 1984 Naganoken-Seibu Earthquake
properties.description: A powerful earthquake struck western Nagano on September 14, 1984. It triggered massive landslides on Mount Ontake, burying a village.

properties.name: 1993 Kushiro-Oki Earthquake
properties.description: A deep M7.5 earthquake struck offshore Kushiro on January 15, 1993. Despite its large magnitude, the deep focal depth limited surface damage.

properties.name: 1993 Hokkaido Nansei-Oki Earthquake
properties.description: A major earthquake struck southwest of Hokkaido on July 12, 1993. It generated a devastating tsunami that destroyed the town of Okushiri, killing 230 people.

properties.name: 1994 Hokkaido-Toho-Oki Earthquake
properties.description: A great M8.3 earthquake struck off eastern Hokkaido on October 4, 1994. It was one of the largest earthquakes in the region and generated a significant tsunami.

properties.name: 1994 Sanriku-Oki Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.7 earthquake struck offshore the Sanriku coast on December 28, 1994. It generated a tsunami up to 3 meters and was felt across northern Honshu.

properties.name: 1995 Great Hanshin (Kobe) Earthquake
properties.description: The Great Hanshin earthquake devastated Kobe and surrounding areas on January 17, 1995. It killed over 6,400 people and caused $100 billion in damage, fundamentally changing Japan's earthquake preparedness.

properties.name: 2000 Tottori Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.6 earthquake struck Tottori Prefecture on October 6, 2000. Despite its moderate magnitude, it caused significant structural damage to older buildings.

properties.name: 2001 Geiyo Earthquake
properties.description: A deep M6.7 earthquake struck the Geiyo region on March 24, 2001. It was widely felt across western Japan and caused moderate damage.

properties.name: 2003 Miyagi Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.1 earthquake struck offshore Miyagi Prefecture on May 26, 2003. It caused minor damage and small tsunami warnings were issued.

properties.name: 2003 Tokachi-Oki Earthquake
properties.description: A great M8.3 earthquake struck offshore Hokkaido on September 26, 2003. It generated tsunami waves and caused fires at oil storage facilities in Tomakomai.

properties.name: 2004 Niigata Chuetsu Earthquake
properties.description: A devastating M6.8 earthquake struck Niigata Prefecture on October 23, 2004. It triggered numerous landslides, derailed a bullet train, and displaced over 100,000 people.

properties.name: 2004 Kii Peninsula Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.4 earthquake occurred off the Kii Peninsula on September 5, 2004. It was followed by a M7.2 aftershock and generated minor tsunami waves.

properties.name: 2005 Fukuoka Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.6 earthquake struck offshore Fukuoka on March 20, 2005. It caused significant damage in Fukuoka city and on Genkai Island.

properties.name: 2005 Miyagi-Oki Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.2 earthquake struck offshore Miyagi on August 16, 2005. It caused moderate shaking in Sendai and minor tsunami waves.

properties.name: 2007 Noto Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.9 earthquake struck the Noto Peninsula on March 25, 2007. It caused significant damage to traditional wooden buildings in rural areas.

properties.name: 2007 Niigata Chuetsu-Oki Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.8 earthquake struck offshore Niigata on July 16, 2007. It caused a fire and radiation leak at the Kashiwazaki-Kariwa nuclear power plant.

properties.name: 2008 Iwate-Miyagi Nairiku Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.9 earthquake struck inland Iwate on June 14, 2008. It triggered massive landslides and was one of the strongest inland earthquakes in recent Japanese history.

properties.name: 2010 Bonin Islands Earthquake
properties.description: A deep M7.4 earthquake struck near the Bonin Islands on December 22, 2010. No damage or tsunami were reported due to its great depth.

properties.name: 2011 Tohoku Earthquake and Tsunami
properties.description: The Great East Japan Earthquake struck on March 11, 2011, generating a massive tsunami up to 40 meters high. It caused the Fukushima nuclear disaster and killed nearly 20,000 people, making it Japan's deadliest natural disaster in modern history.

properties.name: 2011 Fukushima Aftershock
properties.description: A major M7.1 aftershock struck Fukushima on April 7, 2011. It caused additional damage to the already devastated Tohoku region and brief power outages at the Fukushima plant.

properties.name: 2012 Off-Sanriku Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.3 earthquake struck off the Sanriku coast on December 7, 2012. A tsunami advisory was issued and minor waves were observed along the coast.

properties.name: 2013 Awaji Island Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.3 earthquake struck near Awaji Island on April 13, 2013. It was strongly felt in Osaka and Kobe but caused only minor damage.

properties.name: 2014 Nagano Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.7 earthquake struck northern Nagano on November 22, 2014. It caused significant building collapses in Hakuba village and surrounding areas.

properties.name: 2015 Ogasawara Earthquake
properties.description: A very deep M7.9 earthquake struck near the Ogasawara Islands on May 30, 2015. Despite its large magnitude, the 680 km depth prevented significant damage.

properties.name: 2016 Kumamoto Foreshock
properties.description: A M6.5 foreshock struck Kumamoto on April 14, 2016. It caused significant damage to Kumamoto Castle and surrounding buildings, preceding the larger mainshock.

properties.name: 2016 Kumamoto Earthquake
properties.description: The M7.0 mainshock struck Kumamoto on April 16, 2016, just 28 hours after the foreshock. It killed 267 people, caused massive landslides, and severely damaged Kumamoto Castle.

properties.name: 2016 Tottori Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.6 earthquake struck central Tottori Prefecture on October 21, 2016. It caused moderate damage to buildings and infrastructure.

properties.name: 2018 Osaka Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.1 earthquake struck northern Osaka on June 18, 2018. It killed 6 people, including a child struck by a falling wall, and disrupted transportation across the Kansai region.

properties.name: 2018 Hokkaido Eastern Iburi Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.7 earthquake struck eastern Iburi on September 6, 2018. It triggered a massive landslide in Atsuma and caused a total blackout across Hokkaido affecting 5.3 million people.

properties.name: 2019 Yamagata Offshore Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.7 earthquake struck offshore Yamagata on June 18, 2019. A tsunami advisory was issued and minor waves were recorded along the Sea of Japan coast.

properties.name: 2021 Fukushima Earthquake (February)
properties.description: A M7.1 earthquake struck offshore Fukushima on February 13, 2021, considered an aftershock of the 2011 Tohoku event. It injured over 180 people and caused widespread power outages.

properties.name: 2021 Miyagi Earthquake (March)
properties.description: A M7.2 earthquake struck offshore Miyagi on March 20, 2021. A tsunami advisory was briefly issued for the Miyagi coast.

properties.name: 2022 Fukushima Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.4 earthquake struck offshore Fukushima on March 16, 2022. It caused a Shinkansen derailment, power outages affecting 2 million households, and significant structural damage.

properties.name: 2023 Chiba Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.2 earthquake struck offshore Chiba on May 26, 2023. It caused strong shaking in the Tokyo metropolitan area and brief transportation disruptions.

properties.name: 2024 Noto Peninsula Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.6 earthquake struck the Noto Peninsula on January 1, 2024. It caused widespread destruction in Wajima and Suzu, triggered tsunami waves, fires, and killed at least 245 people.

properties.name: 2024 Hyuganada Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.1 earthquake struck the Hyuganada Sea on August 8, 2024. It prompted Japan's first-ever Nankai Trough mega-earthquake advisory.

properties.name: 1976 Irian Jaya Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.1 earthquake struck Irian Jaya on June 25, 1976. It triggered landslides in the remote highlands and killed over 400 people.

properties.name: 1977 Sumba Earthquake
properties.description: A great M8.3 earthquake struck south of Sumba Island on August 19, 1977. It generated a destructive tsunami that devastated coastal communities.

properties.name: 1992 Flores Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.8 earthquake struck Flores Island on December 12, 1992. The resulting tsunami destroyed the town of Maumere, killing about 2,500 people.

properties.name: 2004 Indian Ocean Earthquake and Tsunami
properties.description: The catastrophic M9.1 megathrust earthquake struck off Sumatra on December 26, 2004. It generated a devastating Indian Ocean tsunami that killed approximately 228,000 people across 14 countries, making it one of the deadliest natural disasters in recorded history.

properties.name: 2005 Nias Earthquake
properties.description: A M8.6 earthquake struck off Nias Island on March 28, 2005. It was the third-largest earthquake recorded at the time and caused significant destruction on Nias and Simeulue islands.

properties.name: 2006 Yogyakarta Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.3 earthquake struck near Yogyakarta on May 27, 2006. Despite its moderate magnitude, it killed over 5,700 people due to vulnerable building construction and high population density.

properties.name: 2006 Pangandaran Earthquake and Tsunami
properties.description: A M7.7 tsunami earthquake struck south of Java on July 17, 2006. The slow-rupturing earthquake generated a disproportionately large tsunami that hit the coast of Pangandaran.

properties.name: 2007 Sumatra Earthquakes
properties.description: A M8.5 earthquake struck offshore Sumatra on September 12, 2007, followed by a M7.9 aftershock. The events caused moderate damage along the coast of Bengkulu province.

properties.name: 2009 Padang Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.6 earthquake struck near Padang on September 30, 2009. It caused widespread building collapses in Padang city and killed over 1,100 people.

properties.name: 2010 Mentawai Earthquake and Tsunami
properties.description: A M7.8 tsunami earthquake struck the Mentawai Islands on October 25, 2010. It generated a destructive tsunami up to 7 meters that devastated coastal villages.

properties.name: 2012 Indian Ocean Earthquake
properties.description: A M8.6 strike-slip earthquake struck off Sumatra on April 11, 2012. It was the largest strike-slip earthquake ever recorded and was followed by a M8.2 aftershock.

properties.name: 2016 Aceh Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.5 earthquake struck Pidie Jaya in Aceh on December 7, 2016. It caused significant structural damage and killed over 100 people in the region.

properties.name: 2018 Lombok Earthquake (July)
properties.description: A M6.4 earthquake struck Lombok on July 29, 2018. It was a foreshock to the larger August event and caused damage in northern Lombok.

properties.name: 2018 Lombok Earthquake (August)
properties.description: A M6.9 earthquake struck Lombok on August 5, 2018. It caused widespread destruction across the island, killing over 560 people and displacing hundreds of thousands.

properties.name: 2018 Sulawesi Earthquake and Tsunami
properties.description: A M7.5 earthquake struck Palu, Sulawesi on September 28, 2018. It triggered a devastating tsunami, liquefaction, and landslides, killing over 4,300 people in Palu and Donggala.

properties.name: 2019 Maluku Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.5 earthquake struck Ambon in Maluku on September 26, 2019. It triggered landslides and killed 41 people across the island.

properties.name: 2019 Halmahera Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.3 earthquake struck south of Halmahera on July 14, 2019. It caused significant damage and forced thousands to evacuate.

properties.name: 2021 Sulawesi Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.2 earthquake struck Mamuju, West Sulawesi on January 15, 2021. It caused widespread building collapses including a hospital and government buildings.

properties.name: 2022 Cianjur Earthquake
properties.description: A M5.6 earthquake struck Cianjur, West Java on November 21, 2022. Despite its moderate magnitude, the shallow depth and vulnerable construction led to 602 deaths.

properties.name: 2009 Manokwari Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.6 earthquake struck near Manokwari on January 3, 2009. It caused moderate damage in the city and triggered landslides.

properties.name: 2000 Sumatra Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.9 earthquake struck southern Sumatra on June 4, 2000. It caused significant damage in Bengkulu province and was felt across the region.

properties.name: 1994 Java Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.8 earthquake struck south of Java on June 2, 1994. The resulting tsunami hit the coast of Banyuwangi and killed approximately 250 people.

properties.name: 2021 Flores Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.4 earthquake struck near Flores on December 14, 2021. A tsunami warning was issued but later cancelled after minor waves were recorded.

properties.name: 2023 Maluku Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.1 earthquake struck the Banda Sea near Maluku on January 9, 2023. It was widely felt but its depth limited damage.

properties.name: 2004 Nabire Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.0 earthquake struck near Nabire in Papua on February 4, 2004. It caused building collapses and killed 37 people in the remote town.

properties.name: 1985 Santiago Earthquake
properties.description: A M8.0 earthquake struck central Chile on March 3, 1985. It caused significant damage in Santiago and Valparaiso, killing 177 people.

properties.name: 1995 Antofagasta Earthquake
properties.description: A M8.0 earthquake struck northern Chile on July 30, 1995. The relatively low death toll was attributed to the region's sparse population.

properties.name: 2005 Tarapaca Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.8 earthquake struck the Tarapaca region on June 13, 2005. It caused damage in Iquique and triggered landslides in the Andes.

properties.name: 2007 Tocopilla Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.7 earthquake struck near Tocopilla on November 14, 2007. It damaged over 15,000 homes and displaced thousands of residents.

properties.name: 2010 Maule Earthquake
properties.description: A M8.8 earthquake struck central Chile on February 27, 2010. It was one of the most powerful earthquakes ever recorded and generated a Pacific-wide tsunami, killing 525 people.

properties.name: 2014 Iquique Earthquake
properties.description: A M8.2 earthquake struck offshore Iquique on April 1, 2014. It partially filled the northern Chile seismic gap and generated a moderate tsunami.

properties.name: 2015 Illapel Earthquake
properties.description: A M8.3 earthquake struck offshore Illapel on September 16, 2015. It generated a tsunami up to 4.7 meters and prompted the evacuation of a million people.

properties.name: 2016 Chiloé Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.6 earthquake struck near Chiloé Island on December 25, 2016. It caused moderate damage and triggered landslides but no fatalities were reported.

properties.name: 2010 Pichilemu Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.0 aftershock of the Maule earthquake struck near Pichilemu on March 11, 2010. It caused additional damage to already weakened structures.

properties.name: 1976 Chile Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.3 earthquake struck the Atacama region on December 9, 1976. It caused moderate damage in the mining communities of northern Chile.

properties.name: 2017 Valparaiso Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.9 earthquake struck central Chile on April 24, 2017. It was felt in Santiago and Valparaiso but caused only minor damage.

properties.name: 2019 Coquimbo Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.7 earthquake struck offshore Coquimbo on January 19, 2019. It triggered brief tsunami concern but no significant waves materialized.

properties.name: 2022 Atacama Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.8 earthquake struck the Atacama region on September 18, 2022. It was widely felt across northern Chile.

properties.name: 2001 Southern Peru Earthquake (felt in Chile)
properties.description: The M8.4 Arequipa earthquake in Peru on June 23, 2001 was strongly felt in northern Chile. It caused some damage in Arica and triggered a tsunami along the Chilean coast.

properties.name: 2024 Antofagasta Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.0 earthquake struck offshore Antofagasta on August 11, 2024. A brief tsunami advisory was issued for the northern Chilean coast.

properties.name: 1976 Çaldıran-Muradiye Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.2 earthquake struck the Çaldıran district of Van on November 24, 1976. It destroyed numerous villages and killed nearly 4,000 people in eastern Turkey.

properties.name: 1983 Erzurum Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.9 earthquake struck Horasan-Narman in Erzurum on October 30, 1983. It killed over 1,300 people and destroyed thousands of buildings in eastern Turkey.

properties.name: 1992 Erzincan Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.7 earthquake struck Erzincan on March 13, 1992. It caused widespread damage in the city center and killed nearly 500 people.

properties.name: 1995 Dinar Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.4 earthquake struck the town of Dinar on October 1, 1995. It destroyed much of the town center and displaced thousands of residents.

properties.name: 1998 Adana-Ceyhan Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.3 earthquake struck the Ceyhan district of Adana on June 27, 1998. It caused significant building collapses and killed 145 people.

properties.name: 1999 İzmit Earthquake
properties.description: The devastating M7.6 earthquake struck İzmit on August 17, 1999. It ruptured 150 km of the North Anatolian Fault, killing over 17,000 people and causing $6.5 billion in damage across Turkey's industrial heartland.

properties.name: 1999 Düzce Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.2 earthquake struck Düzce on November 12, 1999, just 87 days after the İzmit earthquake. It extended the rupture further east and killed 894 more people.

properties.name: 2003 Bingöl Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.4 earthquake struck Bingöl on May 1, 2003. A school dormitory collapsed killing many students, accounting for most of the 176 fatalities.

properties.name: 2011 Van Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.1 earthquake struck Erciş and Van on October 23, 2011. It collapsed hundreds of buildings and killed 604 people in eastern Turkey.

properties.name: 2011 Van Earthquake (November)
properties.description: A M5.7 aftershock struck Van on November 9, 2011. Despite its moderate magnitude, it collapsed buildings already weakened by the October earthquake, killing 40 people.

properties.name: 2020 Elazığ Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.7 earthquake struck Sivrice, Elazığ on January 24, 2020. It caused significant damage in Elazığ and Malatya provinces, killing 41 people.

properties.name: 2020 Aegean Sea Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.0 earthquake struck the Aegean Sea on October 30, 2020. It caused devastation in İzmir, Turkey and on the Greek island of Samos, killing 119 people.

properties.name: 2023 Kahramanmaraş Earthquake (First)
properties.description: The catastrophic M7.8 earthquake struck southeastern Turkey on February 6, 2023 at 04:17 local time. It was the deadliest earthquake in Turkey's modern history, with a combined death toll exceeding 53,000 across Turkey and Syria.

properties.name: 2023 Kahramanmaraş Earthquake (Second)
properties.description: A second M7.7 earthquake struck Elbistan, Kahramanmaraş just 9 hours after the first on February 6, 2023. This unusual doublet compounded the destruction across 11 provinces.

properties.name: 2023 Hatay Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.4 earthquake struck Hatay on February 20, 2023, two weeks after the devastating mainshocks. It caused additional collapses of already damaged buildings.

properties.name: 1999 Marmara Sea Aftershock
properties.description: A M5.8 aftershock of the İzmit earthquake struck in the Marmara Sea on September 20, 1999. It caused panic in Istanbul but limited additional damage.

properties.name: 1978 Tabas Earthquake
properties.description: A devastating M7.4 earthquake struck the city of Tabas on September 16, 1978. It virtually destroyed the ancient city and killed approximately 15,000 of its 17,000 inhabitants.

properties.name: 1981 Golbaf Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.1 earthquake struck Golbaf, Kerman Province on June 11, 1981. It caused catastrophic damage and killed approximately 3,000 people.

properties.name: 1990 Manjil-Rudbar Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.4 earthquake struck the Rudbar-Manjil area on June 20, 1990. It was Iran's deadliest 20th century earthquake, killing approximately 40,000 people and leaving 500,000 homeless.

properties.name: 1997 Qayen Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.2 earthquake struck the Qayen region on May 10, 1997. It destroyed several villages and killed over 1,500 people in northeastern Iran.

properties.name: 2003 Bam Earthquake
properties.description: A devastating M6.6 earthquake struck the ancient city of Bam on December 26, 2003. It killed over 26,000 people and destroyed the 2,000-year-old Arg-e Bam citadel, a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

properties.name: 2005 Zarand Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.4 earthquake struck near Zarand on February 22, 2005. It destroyed several villages in the mountainous region and killed over 600 people.

properties.name: 2006 Borujerd Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.1 earthquake struck near Borujerd in Lorestan Province on March 31, 2006. It caused significant damage to rural communities and killed 66 people.

properties.name: 2012 Ahar Earthquake
properties.description: Twin earthquakes of M6.4 and M6.3 struck near Ahar on August 11, 2012. They destroyed several villages in East Azerbaijan Province and killed 306 people.

properties.name: 2013 Bushehr Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.3 earthquake struck near the Bushehr nuclear power plant on April 9, 2013. It caused concern about nuclear safety, though the plant was undamaged.

properties.name: 2017 Kermanshah Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.3 earthquake struck the Iran-Iraq border near Sarpol-e Zahab on November 12, 2017. It killed over 630 people, mostly in Iran, and destroyed the town of Sarpol-e Zahab.

properties.name: 2019 East Azerbaijan Earthquake
properties.description: A M5.9 earthquake struck East Azerbaijan Province on November 8, 2019. It injured over 70 people and damaged buildings in several villages.

properties.name: 2021 Hormozgan Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.3 earthquake struck Hormozgan Province on November 14, 2021. It caused significant damage in Bandar-e Khamir and nearby villages.

properties.name: 2022 Hormozgan Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.0 earthquake struck Hormozgan Province on July 1, 2022. It caused damage in the port city of Bandar Abbas and surrounding areas.

properties.name: 1976 Tangshan Earthquake
properties.description: The catastrophic M7.5 earthquake struck the industrial city of Tangshan on July 28, 1976. It killed approximately 242,000 people, making it one of the deadliest earthquakes in human history, and leveled 85% of the city.

properties.name: 1976 Songpan-Pingwu Earthquakes
properties.description: A series of strong earthquakes (M7.2, 6.7, 7.2) struck Songpan-Pingwu in Sichuan from August 16-23, 1976. They caused significant damage in the mountainous region.

properties.name: 1988 Lancang-Gengma Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.0 earthquake struck Lancang and Gengma counties in Yunnan on November 6, 1988. It killed 748 people and destroyed thousands of buildings.

properties.name: 1996 Lijiang Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.0 earthquake struck near the historic city of Lijiang on February 3, 1996. It killed 309 people and damaged the UNESCO old town.

properties.name: 1999 Jiji Earthquake (felt in Fujian)
properties.description: The M7.7 Jiji earthquake in Taiwan on September 21, 1999 was felt across the Taiwan Strait. Tremors were reported in Fujian Province.

properties.name: 2001 Kunlun Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.8 earthquake struck the Kunlun fault in remote Qinghai on November 14, 2001. It produced the longest surface rupture observed in the 20th century at 450 km.

properties.name: 2008 Sichuan Earthquake
properties.description: The devastating M7.9 earthquake struck Wenchuan County on May 12, 2008. It killed nearly 88,000 people and left 4.8 million homeless, with the collapse of poorly constructed schools drawing widespread criticism.

properties.name: 2010 Yushu Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.9 earthquake struck Yushu on April 14, 2010. It destroyed 85% of buildings in the remote Tibetan town and killed nearly 2,700 people.

properties.name: 2013 Lushan Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.6 earthquake struck Lushan County on April 20, 2013. It occurred on the same fault system as the 2008 Sichuan earthquake and killed 196 people.

properties.name: 2013 Dingxi Earthquake
properties.description: A M5.9 earthquake struck Dingxi in Gansu Province on July 22, 2013. It caused significant damage to rural mud-brick homes and killed 95 people.

properties.name: 2014 Ludian Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.1 earthquake struck Ludian County in Yunnan on August 3, 2014. Despite its moderate magnitude, the shallow depth and poor construction led to 617 deaths.

properties.name: 2014 Yutian Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.3 earthquake struck Yutian County in Xinjiang on February 12, 2014. The remote location and sparse population meant no casualties were reported.

properties.name: 2017 Jiuzhaigou Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.5 earthquake struck near the Jiuzhaigou scenic area on August 8, 2017. It triggered landslides and killed 25 people, including tourists visiting the UNESCO site.

properties.name: 2021 Yangbi Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.4 earthquake struck Yangbi County in Yunnan on May 21, 2021. It caused significant building damage and killed 3 people.

properties.name: 2022 Luding Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.6 earthquake struck Luding County in Sichuan on September 5, 2022. It triggered landslides and killed 93 people in the mountainous region near the Tibetan Plateau.

properties.name: 2023 Jishishan Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.2 earthquake struck Jishishan County in Gansu on December 18, 2023. It killed 151 people in freezing conditions and affected neighboring Qinghai Province.

properties.name: 1988 Nepal-India Border Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.9 earthquake struck the Nepal-India border on August 21, 1988. It killed over 1,000 people and caused widespread damage in eastern Nepal and Bihar, India.

properties.name: 2011 Sikkim Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.9 earthquake struck the Nepal-India border region on September 18, 2011. It killed 111 people across Nepal, India, and Tibet.

properties.name: 2015 Gorkha Earthquake
properties.description: The devastating M7.8 earthquake struck Nepal on April 25, 2015. It killed nearly 9,000 people, destroyed over 600,000 structures, and triggered a deadly avalanche on Mount Everest.

properties.name: 2015 Dolakha Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.3 aftershock struck east of Kathmandu on May 12, 2015. It caused additional devastation and landslides, killing 218 more people in the already ravaged region.

properties.name: 2023 Jajarkot Earthquake
properties.description: A M5.7 earthquake struck Jajarkot in western Nepal on November 3, 2023. The shallow earthquake killed 154 people and destroyed thousands of homes in remote mountain villages.

properties.name: 1987 Edgecumbe Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.5 earthquake struck near Edgecumbe on March 2, 1987. It caused significant damage to the town and was the most damaging NZ earthquake in 56 years.

properties.name: 2009 Fiordland Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.8 earthquake struck Fiordland on July 15, 2009. It was the largest NZ earthquake since 1931 but caused no damage due to its remote location.

properties.name: 2010 Darfield (Canterbury) Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.1 earthquake struck near Darfield on September 4, 2010. It caused significant damage in Christchurch but remarkably no deaths, and revealed previously unknown faults.

properties.name: 2011 Christchurch Earthquake
properties.description: A devastating M6.2 earthquake struck Christchurch on February 22, 2011. It killed 185 people and destroyed much of the city center, including the iconic cathedral.

properties.name: 2013 Seddon Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.5 earthquake struck near Seddon on August 16, 2013. It caused moderate damage in Wellington and the Marlborough region.

properties.name: 2016 Kaikōura Earthquake
properties.description: A complex M7.8 earthquake ruptured multiple faults near Kaikōura on November 14, 2016. It raised the seabed by up to 6 meters, triggered massive landslides, and severed road and rail links.

properties.name: 2021 Kermadec Islands Earthquake
properties.description: A M8.1 earthquake struck near the Kermadec Islands on March 4, 2021. It triggered tsunami warnings across the Pacific and evacuations along New Zealand's east coast.

properties.name: 2023 Taranaki Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.2 earthquake struck offshore Taranaki on January 10, 2023. It was widely felt across the North Island but caused no significant damage.

properties.name: 1985 Mexico City Earthquake
properties.description: The devastating M8.0 earthquake struck offshore Michoacán on September 19, 1985. The resulting ground amplification in Mexico City's lake-bed soil killed approximately 10,000 people and collapsed over 400 buildings.

properties.name: 1995 Colima-Jalisco Earthquake
properties.description: A M8.0 earthquake struck the coast of Colima-Jalisco on October 9, 1995. It was the largest Mexican earthquake since 1932 and generated a local tsunami.

properties.name: 1999 Oaxaca Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.4 earthquake struck Oaxaca on September 30, 1999. It caused significant damage in the state capital and killed 35 people.

properties.name: 2003 Colima Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.5 earthquake struck near Colima on January 22, 2003. It caused widespread damage along the Pacific coast and killed 29 people.

properties.name: 2010 Baja California Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.2 earthquake struck northern Baja California on April 4, 2010. It was felt across the US border in California and caused moderate damage in Mexicali.

properties.name: 2012 Guerrero-Oaxaca Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.4 earthquake struck Guerrero on March 20, 2012. It was strongly felt in Mexico City and caused moderate damage along the coast.

properties.name: 2017 Chiapas Earthquake
properties.description: A M8.2 earthquake struck offshore Chiapas on September 8, 2017. It was the strongest earthquake in Mexico in a century and killed 98 people.

properties.name: 2017 Puebla Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.1 earthquake struck near Puebla on September 19, 2017, exactly 32 years after the 1985 earthquake. It caused devastating building collapses in Mexico City, killing 370 people.

properties.name: 2020 Oaxaca Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.4 earthquake struck Oaxaca on June 23, 2020. It was widely felt in Mexico City and generated a minor tsunami along the coast.

properties.name: 2022 Michoacán Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.6 earthquake struck Michoacán on September 19, 2022, remarkably the third major earthquake on this date in Mexico. It caused moderate damage and triggered a small tsunami.

properties.name: 2005 Kashmir Earthquake
properties.description: The devastating M7.6 earthquake struck Kashmir on October 8, 2005. It killed over 86,000 people, injured 69,000, and left 3.5 million homeless, primarily destroying the city of Muzaffarabad.

properties.name: 2008 Balochistan Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.4 earthquake struck Ziarat in Balochistan on October 29, 2008. It destroyed the historic Quaid-e-Azam Residency and killed 166 people.

properties.name: 2011 Balochistan Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.2 earthquake struck southwestern Balochistan on January 18, 2011. It was widely felt across the region but the sparse population limited casualties.

properties.name: 2013 Balochistan Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.7 earthquake struck Awaran in Balochistan on September 24, 2013. It was so powerful it created a new island off the coast of Gwadar through mud volcano eruption.

properties.name: 2015 Hindu Kush Earthquake
properties.description: A deep M7.5 earthquake struck the Hindu Kush on October 26, 2015. It was felt across Pakistan, Afghanistan, and India, killing nearly 400 people.

properties.name: 2019 Mirpur Earthquake
properties.description: A M5.6 earthquake struck near Mirpur in Azad Kashmir on September 24, 2019. The shallow earthquake caused disproportionate damage, destroying roads and buildings.

properties.name: 2021 Balochistan Earthquake
properties.description: A M5.9 earthquake struck Harnai in Balochistan on October 7, 2021. It collapsed mud-brick houses in rural villages and killed 23 people.

properties.name: 1976 Friuli Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.5 earthquake struck the Friuli region on May 6, 1976. It destroyed numerous villages and killed 989 people, leading to major advances in Italian seismic building codes.

properties.name: 1980 Irpinia Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.9 earthquake struck Irpinia in southern Italy on November 23, 1980. It killed nearly 3,000 people and exposed systemic failures in Italian emergency response.

properties.name: 2009 L'Aquila Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.3 earthquake struck L'Aquila on April 6, 2009. It killed 308 people and devastated the medieval city center, leading to a controversial trial of seismologists.

properties.name: 2012 Emilia Earthquake

properties.name: 2016 Amatrice Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.2 earthquake struck Amatrice and surrounding towns on August 24, 2016. It virtually destroyed the medieval town and killed 299 people, mostly in collapsed stone buildings.

properties.name: 2016 Norcia Earthquake

properties.name: 2017 Ischia Earthquake

properties.name: 1989 Loma Prieta Earthquake

properties.name: 1992 Landers Earthquake

properties.name: 1994 Northridge Earthquake

properties.name: 2002 Denali Earthquake

properties.name: 2018 Anchorage Earthquake

properties.name: 2019 Ridgecrest Earthquakes

properties.description: A M7.8 earthquake struck near Haida Gwaii (Queen Charlotte Islands) on October 28, 2012. It generated a small tsunami and was the largest Canadian earthquake since 1949.

properties.name: 2018 Lombok Earthquake (August 19)
properties.description: A M6.9 earthquake struck eastern Lombok on August 19, 2018, just two weeks after the previous major event. It caused further destruction on the already devastated island.

properties.name: 2011 Nagano Earthquake (March)
properties.description: A M6.7 earthquake struck northern Nagano on March 12, 2011, one day after the Tohoku megaquake. It caused building collapses in the Sakae village area.

properties.name: 2011 Sikkim Earthquake (India)
properties.description: A M6.9 earthquake struck Sikkim on September 18, 2011. It caused widespread damage in the mountainous state and killed 97 people in India alone.

properties.name: 2024 Noto Aftershock
properties.description: A M6.3 aftershock of the Noto earthquake struck on January 1, 2024. It compounded damage in the already devastated peninsula region.

properties.name: 2014 Iquique Aftershock
properties.description: A M7.7 aftershock struck just two days after the mainshock on April 3, 2014. It was one of the largest aftershocks recorded globally.

properties.name: 1999 Kocaeli Foreshock
properties.description: A M6.0 earthquake was part of the İzmit sequence in August 1999. It contributed to damage in the Yalova area south of the main rupture.

properties.name: 2015 Molucca Sea Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.0 earthquake struck the Molucca Sea on November 4, 2015. It was felt across the region but its depth limited damage.

properties.name: 2005 Kashmir Aftershock
properties.description: A M6.4 aftershock struck on October 8, 2005, the same day as the mainshock. It compounded the destruction in the Kashmir region.

properties.name: 2007 Gisborne Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.7 earthquake struck near Gisborne on December 20, 2007. It caused moderate damage in the city, including the partial collapse of a building.

properties.name: 1980 Azores Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.9 earthquake struck the Azores on January 1, 1980. It killed 61 people and caused widespread damage on Terceira Island.

properties.name: 1979 Montenegro Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.0 earthquake struck the Adriatic coast on April 15, 1979. It devastated the city of Budva and killed 129 people across Montenegro and Albania.

properties.name: 2006 Kurile Islands Earthquake (Japan impact)
properties.description: A M7.0 earthquake struck the Kurile Islands chain on January 2, 2006. Minor tsunami waves were observed along the Hokkaido coast.

properties.name: 2008 Kyrgyzstan Earthquake
properties.description: A M6.6 earthquake struck the Nura area in southern Kyrgyzstan on October 5, 2008. It triggered a massive landslide that buried the village and killed 72 people.

properties.name: 1994 Sanriku-Haruka-oki Earthquake
properties.description: A M7.5 earthquake struck far offshore the Sanriku coast on December 28, 1994. A small tsunami was recorded along the northern Honshu coast.

properties.name: Hurricane David
properties.description: Hurricane David was one of the deadliest Atlantic hurricanes of the late 20th century. It devastated Dominica and the Dominican Republic before striking the US East Coast as a weakened but still dangerous storm.

properties.name: Hurricane Allen
properties.description: Hurricane Allen was one of the strongest Atlantic hurricanes ever recorded, reaching peak winds of 305 km/h. It caused widespread destruction across the Caribbean and made landfall in southern Texas.

properties.name: Hurricane Gilbert
properties.description: Hurricane Gilbert was the most intense Atlantic hurricane on record at the time with a pressure of 888 mbar. It devastated Jamaica, the Cayman Islands, and northeastern Mexico.

properties.name: Hurricane Hugo
properties.description: Hurricane Hugo was a powerful Category 4 hurricane that struck South Carolina near Charleston. It previously devastated Guadeloupe, Montserrat, and the US Virgin Islands.

properties.name: Hurricane Andrew
properties.description: Hurricane Andrew was a compact but devastating Category 5 hurricane that struck Homestead, Florida, causing $27 billion in damage. It crossed the Gulf and made a second landfall in Louisiana.

properties.name: Hurricane Opal
properties.description: Hurricane Opal rapidly intensified over the Gulf of Mexico to Category 4 before making landfall near Pensacola Beach, Florida. It caused extensive storm surge damage along the Gulf Coast.

properties.name: Hurricane Fran
properties.description: Hurricane Fran made landfall near Cape Fear, North Carolina, as a Category 3 hurricane. It caused extensive wind and flood damage across the state, with effects reaching into Virginia and West Virginia.

properties.name: Hurricane Georges
properties.description: Hurricane Georges struck multiple Caribbean islands as a Category 4 hurricane, causing catastrophic damage in the Dominican Republic, Haiti, and Puerto Rico before hitting the US Gulf Coast.

properties.name: Hurricane Mitch
properties.description: Hurricane Mitch was one of the deadliest Atlantic hurricanes in history, causing catastrophic flooding and mudslides across Honduras and Nicaragua. The death toll exceeded 11,000, mostly from flooding.

properties.name: Hurricane Floyd
properties.description: Hurricane Floyd prompted the largest peacetime evacuation in US history with 2.6 million people ordered to leave coastal areas. It caused catastrophic flooding in eastern North Carolina.

properties.name: Hurricane Keith
properties.description: Hurricane Keith stalled over the Belize barrier reef, causing extreme rainfall and flooding. It struck Belize City and caused significant agricultural damage across Central America.

properties.name: Tropical Storm Debby
properties.description: Tropical Storm Debby caused significant flooding and mudslides across the Lesser Antilles, particularly impacting Saint Lucia. The storm brought heavy rains to several Caribbean islands.

properties.name: Tropical Storm Allison
properties.description: Tropical Storm Allison caused catastrophic flooding in Houston, Texas, dropping nearly 1 meter of rain in some areas. It was the costliest tropical storm in US history at the time, causing $9 billion in damage.

properties.name: Hurricane Michelle
properties.description: Hurricane Michelle was the strongest hurricane to hit Cuba since 1952. It caused extensive damage to agriculture and infrastructure across western Cuba, though timely evacuations limited casualties.

properties.name: Hurricane Isidore
properties.description: Hurricane Isidore made landfall on the Yucatan Peninsula as a Category 3 hurricane before weakening and making a second landfall in Louisiana. It brought devastating storm surge to Progreso, Mexico.

properties.name: Hurricane Lili
properties.description: Hurricane Lili rapidly intensified to Category 4 in the Gulf of Mexico before weakening slightly prior to landfall in Louisiana. It caused significant sugar cane crop damage and power outages.

properties.name: Hurricane Isabel
properties.description: Hurricane Isabel was the most significant hurricane to affect the Mid-Atlantic region since 1979. It peaked as a Category 5 over the open Atlantic before making landfall in North Carolina as Category 2.

properties.name: Hurricane Fabian
properties.description: Hurricane Fabian was the worst hurricane to hit Bermuda in 50 years, passing directly over the island as a Category 3 storm. It caused four fatalities on the island and extensive infrastructure damage.

properties.name: Hurricane Charley
properties.description: Hurricane Charley unexpectedly intensified to Category 4 and made a sharp right turn, striking Punta Gorda, Florida instead of the Tampa Bay area. It was compact but extremely destructive.

properties.name: Hurricane Ivan
properties.description: Hurricane Ivan was a long-lived Cape Verde hurricane that devastated Grenada and caused extreme damage along the US Gulf Coast. It reached Category 5 strength three times during its lifetime.

properties.name: Hurricane Jeanne
properties.description: Hurricane Jeanne caused catastrophic flooding in Gonaives, Haiti, killing over 3,000 people in mudslides. It later struck Florida's Atlantic coast near Stuart as a Category 3 hurricane.

properties.name: Hurricane Dennis
properties.description: Hurricane Dennis was the strongest Atlantic hurricane ever recorded before August at the time. It made landfall in Cuba twice and then struck the Florida Panhandle as a Category 3 hurricane.

properties.name: Hurricane Katrina
properties.description: Hurricane Katrina was the costliest natural disaster in US history and one of the deadliest. Catastrophic storm surge breached the levees in New Orleans, inundating 80% of the city.

properties.name: Hurricane Rita
properties.description: Hurricane Rita was the fourth most intense Atlantic hurricane ever recorded at the time, with a pressure of 895 mbar. It caused massive devastation along the Texas-Louisiana border.

properties.name: Hurricane Wilma
properties.description: Hurricane Wilma set the record for the most intense Atlantic hurricane with a pressure of 882 mbar. It battered the Yucatan Peninsula, then crossed South Florida causing significant damage.

properties.name: Hurricane Ernesto
properties.description: Hurricane Ernesto formed in the Caribbean and tracked northward, briefly reaching hurricane strength near the North Carolina coast. It brought significant flooding to the Mid-Atlantic region.

properties.name: Hurricane Gordon
properties.description: Hurricane Gordon was a powerful Cape Verde hurricane that tracked across the open Atlantic. It passed near the Azores as an extratropical cyclone, bringing hurricane-force winds to the islands.

properties.name: Hurricane Dean
properties.description: Hurricane Dean was the strongest Atlantic hurricane to make landfall since Andrew in 1992. It struck the Yucatan Peninsula at Category 5 intensity, devastating Quintana Roo.

properties.name: Hurricane Felix
properties.description: Hurricane Felix rapidly intensified to Category 5 and struck the Miskito Coast of Nicaragua. It was the first Category 5 hurricane to make landfall since Dean just two weeks earlier.

properties.name: Hurricane Gustav
properties.description: Hurricane Gustav devastated western Cuba as a Category 4 hurricane and caused a massive evacuation of New Orleans three years after Katrina. It made landfall in Louisiana as a Category 2.

properties.name: Hurricane Ike
properties.description: Hurricane Ike was one of the largest Atlantic hurricanes by wind field. It devastated the Turks and Caicos before striking Galveston, Texas, where it caused a massive storm surge.

properties.name: Hurricane Bill
properties.description: Hurricane Bill was the strongest hurricane of the quiet 2009 season, reaching Category 4 over the open Atlantic. Its large swells caused dangerous rip currents along the US East Coast.

properties.name: Hurricane Ida
properties.description: Hurricane Ida was the strongest November hurricane in a decade. After impacting Nicaragua, its remnants caused deadly flooding along the US Mid-Atlantic coast and especially in El Salvador.

properties.name: Hurricane Earl
properties.description: Hurricane Earl was a powerful Cape Verde hurricane that threatened the US East Coast. It passed near the Outer Banks of North Carolina before striking Nova Scotia as a weakened hurricane.

properties.name: Hurricane Tomas
properties.description: Hurricane Tomas caused severe flooding in Haiti and Saint Lucia. In Haiti, the flooding exacerbated a cholera outbreak, greatly increasing the death toll.

properties.name: Hurricane Irene
properties.description: Hurricane Irene was a large and destructive hurricane that struck the Outer Banks of North Carolina before making a second landfall in New Jersey. It caused massive inland flooding in Vermont.

properties.name: Tropical Storm Lee
properties.description: Tropical Storm Lee produced catastrophic flooding across the Mid-Atlantic and Northeast when its moisture combined with Hurricane Katia's remnants. It caused record flooding in Pennsylvania.

properties.name: Hurricane Isaac
properties.description: Hurricane Isaac made landfall near the mouth of the Mississippi River on the 7th anniversary of Hurricane Katrina. Its slow movement caused extreme rainfall and flooding in Louisiana.

properties.name: Hurricane Sandy
properties.description: Hurricane Sandy was the largest Atlantic hurricane on record by diameter. It made a devastating left turn into New Jersey, causing catastrophic storm surge flooding in New York City and the Jersey Shore.

properties.name: Hurricane Ingrid
properties.description: Hurricane Ingrid made landfall on the eastern coast of Mexico near La Pesca, Tamaulipas. Combined with Pacific Tropical Storm Manuel, the dual storms caused deadly flooding across Mexico.

properties.name: Hurricane Humberto
properties.description: Hurricane Humberto was notable for being the first hurricane of the quiet 2013 season, not forming until September 11. It became the latest first hurricane in a season since 2002.

properties.name: Hurricane Arthur
properties.description: Hurricane Arthur was the earliest hurricane to make landfall in North Carolina since 1959. It struck the Outer Banks on July 4th, disrupting holiday celebrations.

properties.name: Hurricane Gonzalo
properties.description: Hurricane Gonzalo was the strongest hurricane to strike Bermuda since Fabian in 2003. It passed directly over the island as a Category 2, causing significant damage to infrastructure.

properties.name: Hurricane Joaquin
properties.description: Hurricane Joaquin stalled over the Bahamas as a Category 4 hurricane, devastating the central islands. The cargo ship El Faro sank in the storm, killing all 33 crew members.

properties.name: Tropical Storm Erika
properties.description: Tropical Storm Erika caused devastating flash floods and mudslides in Dominica, killing 31 people and causing damage equivalent to 90% of GDP. The island community of Petite Savanne was destroyed.

properties.name: Hurricane Matthew
properties.description: Hurricane Matthew was the first Category 5 Atlantic hurricane since Felix in 2007. It devastated southwestern Haiti, causing catastrophic flooding and mudslides that killed over 500 people.

properties.name: Hurricane Otto
properties.description: Hurricane Otto was the southernmost landfalling hurricane in Central America on record and the latest Category 3 hurricane in an Atlantic season. It crossed into the Pacific after making landfall.

properties.name: Hurricane Harvey
properties.description: Hurricane Harvey stalled over Southeast Texas after making landfall near Rockport, producing unprecedented rainfall totals exceeding 1.5 meters in some locations. It caused $125 billion in damage.

properties.name: Hurricane Irma
properties.description: Hurricane Irma maintained Category 5 intensity for 60 consecutive hours, a record in the satellite era. It devastated Barbuda, Saint Martin, and the US Virgin Islands before striking the Florida Keys.

properties.name: Hurricane Maria
properties.description: Hurricane Maria devastated Puerto Rico as a Category 4 hurricane, causing a complete collapse of the power grid and estimated 2,975 excess deaths. It also destroyed Dominica at Category 5 intensity.

properties.name: Hurricane Florence
properties.description: Hurricane Florence stalled near the Carolina coast, producing catastrophic rainfall and flooding. Some areas received over 900 mm of rain, causing devastating inland flooding in the Carolinas.

properties.name: Hurricane Michael
properties.description: Hurricane Michael was the first Category 5 hurricane to strike the Florida Panhandle and the strongest on record. It devastated Mexico Beach and Tyndall Air Force Base with extreme storm surge and winds.

properties.name: Hurricane Dorian
properties.description: Hurricane Dorian stalled over Grand Bahama and Great Abaco for nearly two days at Category 5 intensity, causing total destruction. It was the strongest hurricane to strike the Bahamas on record.

properties.name: Hurricane Lorenzo
properties.description: Hurricane Lorenzo was the easternmost and northernmost Category 5 hurricane in the Atlantic basin on record. Its remnants later struck Ireland and the United Kingdom with heavy rain and wind.

properties.name: Hurricane Laura
properties.description: Hurricane Laura tied the record for strongest hurricane to make landfall in Louisiana, striking near Cameron with 240 km/h winds. It caused catastrophic wind damage in Lake Charles.

properties.name: Hurricane Eta
properties.description: Hurricane Eta made landfall in northeastern Nicaragua as a Category 4 hurricane, causing catastrophic flooding across Central America. It later tracked through the Caribbean and impacted Florida.

properties.name: Hurricane Iota
properties.description: Hurricane Iota was the strongest hurricane of the record-setting 2020 season. It struck Nicaragua just 15 miles from where Eta had hit two weeks earlier, compounding the devastation.

properties.name: Hurricane Ida
properties.description: Hurricane Ida made landfall on the 16th anniversary of Katrina as a powerful Category 4 in Port Fourchon, Louisiana. Its remnants caused deadly flash flooding in New York and New Jersey.

properties.name: Hurricane Elsa
properties.description: Hurricane Elsa was the earliest fifth named storm on record in the Atlantic. It caused significant flooding across the Lesser Antilles and made landfall on Florida's Gulf Coast.

properties.name: Hurricane Fiona
properties.description: Hurricane Fiona caused island-wide power outages in Puerto Rico and catastrophic flooding in the Dominican Republic. It later became the strongest hurricane to strike Atlantic Canada on record.

properties.name: Hurricane Ian
properties.description: Hurricane Ian made landfall near Cayo Costa, Florida, as one of the strongest hurricanes to hit the US. It caused catastrophic storm surge in Fort Myers and devastating flooding across Central Florida.

properties.name: Hurricane Lee
properties.description: Hurricane Lee rapidly intensified to Category 5 in the open Atlantic, reaching peak winds of 270 km/h. It later struck Nova Scotia and New Brunswick as a large post-tropical cyclone.

properties.name: Hurricane Idalia
properties.description: Hurricane Idalia rapidly intensified in the Gulf of Mexico and made landfall in Florida's Big Bend region at Category 3 intensity. It was the strongest hurricane to hit that area since 1896.

properties.name: Hurricane Beryl
properties.description: Hurricane Beryl was the earliest Category 5 hurricane in Atlantic history, forming in late June. It devastated Carriacou and the Windward Islands before later making landfall in Texas.

properties.name: Hurricane Helene
properties.description: Hurricane Helene made landfall in Florida's Big Bend region and caused catastrophic inland flooding across the Appalachian Mountains. Western North Carolina was devastated, with Asheville suffering historic flooding.

properties.name: Hurricane Milton
properties.description: Hurricane Milton rapidly intensified to Category 5 in the Gulf of Mexico, becoming one of the strongest Atlantic hurricanes by pressure. It struck Florida's Gulf Coast near Siesta Key as a Category 3.

properties.name: Hurricane Erin
properties.description: Hurricane Erin was a powerful major hurricane in August 2025 that reached Category 4 strength with 140-knot winds and a minimum pressure of 913 mbar in the Atlantic.

properties.name: Hurricane Melissa
properties.description: Hurricane Melissa was the most intense storm of the 2025 Atlantic season with peak winds of 165 knots and a minimum pressure of 892 mbar. It caused significant destruction and loss of life in Jamaica, leading to its name being retired.

properties.name: Typhoon Tip
properties.description: Typhoon Tip was the most intense tropical cyclone ever recorded worldwide, with a minimum pressure of 870 mbar. It was also the largest by diameter, spanning 2,220 km.

properties.name: Typhoon Ike
properties.description: Typhoon Ike struck the Visayas region of the Philippines, causing over 1,400 deaths. The devastation was concentrated in the provinces of Leyte and Samar.

properties.name: Tropical Storm Thelma
properties.description: Tropical Storm Thelma caused catastrophic flash flooding in Ormoc City, Leyte, killing over 5,000 people. It remains one of the deadliest Philippine weather disasters.

properties.name: Typhoon Bart
properties.description: Typhoon Bart struck western Japan as a powerful typhoon, causing storm surge flooding in the Yatsushiro Sea area. It was the strongest typhoon to hit Japan in 8 years.

properties.name: Typhoon Rusa
properties.description: Typhoon Rusa was the most destructive typhoon to hit South Korea since 1959, causing catastrophic flooding that killed 233 people. Gangneung recorded 870 mm of rain in 24 hours.

properties.name: Typhoon Maemi
properties.description: Typhoon Maemi was the most powerful typhoon to strike South Korea since records began. It caused devastating storm surge in Masan and catastrophic wind damage across the southern coast.

properties.name: Typhoon Tokage
properties.description: Typhoon Tokage was the deadliest typhoon to strike Japan in a decade, causing widespread flooding and landslides. It was the tenth typhoon to hit Japan in the record-setting 2004 season.

properties.name: Typhoon Longwang
properties.description: Typhoon Longwang struck Taiwan and southeastern China, causing deadly flooding. Its name was retired due to the significant casualties in Fujian Province, China.

properties.name: Typhoon Saomai
properties.description: Typhoon Saomai was the strongest typhoon to make landfall in mainland China since records began. It struck Zhejiang Province with devastating winds, destroying thousands of fishing boats.

properties.name: Typhoon Durian
properties.description: Typhoon Durian triggered deadly lahar flows from Mayon Volcano that buried entire communities in Albay Province. It was one of the deadliest Philippine typhoons in the 2000s.

properties.name: Typhoon Fengshen
properties.description: Typhoon Fengshen capsized the passenger ferry MV Princess of the Stars off Romblon, killing over 800 people. It caused widespread flooding and destruction across the Visayas and Luzon.

properties.name: Typhoon Morakot
properties.description: Typhoon Morakot dumped nearly 3 meters of rain on southern Taiwan, triggering the catastrophic Xiaolin village mudslide that buried over 400 people. It was Taiwan's deadliest typhoon in 50 years.

properties.name: Typhoon Ketsana
properties.description: Typhoon Ketsana dumped a month's worth of rain on Metro Manila in just six hours, causing the worst flooding in the capital in over 40 years. It went on to devastate Vietnam and Laos.

properties.name: Typhoon Megi
properties.description: Typhoon Megi was the strongest tropical cyclone worldwide in 2010, with peak winds of 295 km/h. It made landfall in northeastern Luzon before hitting southeastern China.

properties.name: Typhoon Bopha
properties.description: Typhoon Bopha was the strongest and deadliest typhoon to strike Mindanao on record. It devastated the Compostela Valley and New Bataan, killing over 1,900 people.

properties.name: Typhoon Haiyan
properties.description: Typhoon Haiyan (Yolanda) was one of the strongest tropical cyclones ever recorded at landfall, with estimated winds of 315 km/h. It caused a catastrophic storm surge in Tacloban City, killing over 6,000 people.

properties.name: Typhoon Rammasun
properties.description: Typhoon Rammasun (Glenda) was the strongest typhoon to hit the Philippines since Haiyan. It then struck southern China as the strongest typhoon there since 1973.

properties.name: Typhoon Melor
properties.description: Typhoon Melor struck the Philippines during the holiday season, making landfall in the Visayas. It caused significant agricultural damage and displaced hundreds of thousands of people.

properties.name: Typhoon Meranti
properties.description: Typhoon Meranti was the strongest tropical cyclone worldwide in 2016, with one-minute sustained winds of 315 km/h. It struck southern Taiwan before making landfall in Xiamen, China.

properties.name: Typhoon Haima
properties.description: Typhoon Haima (Lawin) made landfall in Cagayan province as a Category 5 super typhoon, devastating agricultural areas in the Cagayan Valley. It was the second super typhoon to hit the Philippines in 2016.

properties.name: Typhoon Mangkhut
properties.description: Typhoon Mangkhut (Ompong) was the strongest tropical cyclone of 2018. It devastated northern Luzon with extreme winds and caused deadly landslides in Benguet before striking Hong Kong and southern China.

properties.name: Typhoon Jebi
properties.description: Typhoon Jebi was the strongest typhoon to make landfall in Japan since 1993. It caused major damage in the Kansai region, including flooding Kansai International Airport and toppling a tanker into a bridge.

properties.name: Typhoon Trami
properties.description: Typhoon Trami struck Okinawa and then made landfall on Shikoku and Honshu, bringing destructive winds and storm surge to Japan. It was the second powerful typhoon to hit Japan within a month after Jebi.

properties.name: Typhoon Faxai
properties.description: Typhoon Faxai struck the Tokyo metropolitan area with extreme winds, causing widespread power outages affecting nearly a million homes in Chiba Prefecture. Infrastructure damage was extensive.

properties.name: Typhoon Hagibis
properties.description: Typhoon Hagibis caused catastrophic flooding and landslides across central and eastern Japan, breaking rainfall records. It was the most destructive typhoon in Japan since Ida in 1958.

properties.name: Typhoon Goni
properties.description: Typhoon Goni (Rolly) made landfall in the Philippines as the strongest landfalling tropical cyclone on record globally, with sustained winds of 315 km/h. It devastated Catanduanes and Albay.

properties.name: Typhoon Vamco
properties.description: Typhoon Vamco (Ulysses) caused the worst flooding in Metro Manila since 2009. It triggered massive flooding in the Marikina River basin and Cagayan Valley, with dams forced to release water.

properties.name: Typhoon Rai
properties.description: Typhoon Rai (Odette) was the strongest typhoon to hit the Philippines in 2021, devastating Siargao, Dinagat Islands, and Bohol. It was an unusually powerful late-season storm.

properties.name: Typhoon Noru
properties.description: Typhoon Noru rapidly intensified before striking Luzon, then crossed the South China Sea and made a second landfall in central Vietnam. It caused significant agricultural and infrastructure damage.

properties.name: Typhoon Doksuri
properties.description: Typhoon Doksuri (Egay) was a powerful super typhoon that brought heavy rainfall to the Philippines and then caused catastrophic flooding in Beijing and northern China after making landfall in Fujian.

properties.name: Typhoon Yagi
properties.description: Typhoon Yagi was the strongest typhoon to hit Vietnam in 30 years. It underwent extreme rapid intensification over the South China Sea before devastating Haiphong and triggering catastrophic floods and landslides.

properties.name: Typhoon Gaemi
properties.description: Typhoon Gaemi intensified the southwest monsoon, causing severe flooding in Manila. It then struck Taiwan and southeastern China, causing widespread agricultural damage and flooding.

properties.name: Typhoon Ragasa
properties.description: Super Typhoon Ragasa was the most intense storm of the 2025 Western Pacific season, peaking with sustained winds of 205 km/h (10-min) and a minimum pressure of 905 hPa. It was the only super typhoon of the season.

properties.name: Typhoon Kalmaegi
properties.description: Typhoon Kalmaegi caused the highest death toll of the 2025 Western Pacific typhoon season with over 288 fatalities. Eight names including Kalmaegi were retired due to the severity of damage.

properties.name: Typhoon Matmo
properties.description: Typhoon Matmo was the costliest storm of the 2025 Western Pacific season, causing estimated economic losses of US$3.86 billion. Its name was retired due to severe economic impacts.

properties.name: 1991 Bangladesh Cyclone
properties.description: The 1991 Bangladesh cyclone was one of the deadliest tropical cyclones in history. A 6-meter storm surge inundated the low-lying coastal areas, killing an estimated 138,000 people and leaving 10 million homeless.

properties.name: 1999 Odisha Super Cyclone
properties.description: The 1999 Odisha Super Cyclone was the strongest cyclone to hit India in the 20th century, with a devastating storm surge of 8 meters. It destroyed 2 million homes and killed nearly 10,000 people.

properties.name: Cyclone Sidr
properties.description: Cyclone Sidr was a devastating Category 5 cyclone that struck the Bangladesh coast with a massive storm surge. It was the strongest cyclone to hit Bangladesh since 1991, killing over 3,400 people.

properties.name: Cyclone Nargis
properties.description: Cyclone Nargis was the deadliest cyclone in Myanmar's history and the worst natural disaster in the country's recorded history. A 4-meter storm surge swept through the densely populated Irrawaddy Delta.

properties.name: Cyclone Laila
properties.description: Cyclone Laila made landfall near Bapatla in Andhra Pradesh, causing significant damage to agriculture. Heavy rainfall triggered flooding and mudslides in the state.

properties.name: Cyclone Phailin
properties.description: Cyclone Phailin was the strongest cyclone to hit India since the 1999 Odisha cyclone. Massive pre-emptive evacuations of over 550,000 people dramatically reduced the death toll.

properties.name: Cyclone Hudhud
properties.description: Cyclone Hudhud made landfall near Visakhapatnam as a powerful cyclone. It caused massive destruction in the city and surrounding areas, with damage exceeding $3.4 billion.

properties.name: Cyclone Mora
properties.description: Cyclone Mora made landfall in southeastern Bangladesh near Chittagong, causing extensive flooding and displacement. It affected Rohingya refugee camps in Cox's Bazar district.

properties.name: Cyclone Ockhi
properties.description: Cyclone Ockhi formed near Sri Lanka and rapidly intensified as it moved over the Lakshadweep Sea. It killed hundreds of fishermen from Kerala and Tamil Nadu who were caught at sea.

properties.name: Cyclone Titli
properties.description: Cyclone Titli made landfall near Palasa in Andhra Pradesh and then recurved northward into Odisha, causing catastrophic flooding and landslides in the Gajapati district.

properties.name: Cyclone Fani
properties.description: Cyclone Fani was the strongest cyclone to hit Odisha since 1999 and the strongest April cyclone in the Bay of Bengal since 2008. Pre-emptive evacuation of 1.2 million people saved thousands of lives.

properties.name: Cyclone Bulbul
properties.description: Cyclone Bulbul made landfall on the India-Bangladesh border in the Sundarbans region. Evacuations of over 2 million people in Bangladesh minimized casualties.

properties.name: Cyclone Amphan
properties.description: Cyclone Amphan was the strongest super cyclone in the Bay of Bengal since 1999. It caused catastrophic damage in Kolkata and coastal West Bengal, with estimated losses exceeding $13 billion.

properties.name: Cyclone Nisarga
properties.description: Cyclone Nisarga was the first cyclone to threaten Mumbai since 2009, making landfall south of the city near Alibag. It was a rare Arabian Sea cyclone that struck the Indian west coast.

properties.name: Cyclone Tauktae
properties.description: Cyclone Tauktae was the strongest cyclone to hit Gujarat since 1998. It caused major destruction along the western Indian coast and sank a barge offshore Mumbai, killing dozens of workers.

properties.name: Cyclone Yaas
properties.description: Cyclone Yaas made landfall near Balasore, Odisha, just 12 days after Cyclone Tauktae struck the west coast. It caused significant coastal flooding and damage to the embankment system.

properties.name: Cyclone Biparjoy
properties.description: Cyclone Biparjoy was the longest-lived Arabian Sea cyclone in the satellite era, lasting 14 days. It made landfall near Jakhau in Gujarat as a Category 1 cyclone after weakening.

properties.name: Cyclone Remal
properties.description: Cyclone Remal made landfall on the India-Bangladesh coast near the Sundarbans, causing widespread flooding. It was followed by severe flooding across northeastern India and Bangladesh.

properties.name: 1998 Gujarat Cyclone
properties.description: The 1998 Gujarat cyclone struck the Kandla port area with devastating storm surge, killing approximately 4,000 people. It was the deadliest Arabian Sea cyclone in decades.

properties.name: Cyclone Geralda
properties.description: Cyclone Geralda was one of the most intense tropical cyclones in the Southwest Indian Ocean basin. It struck northeastern Madagascar with devastating winds and flooding.

properties.name: Cyclone Eline
properties.description: Cyclone Eline was a long-lived and destructive cyclone that struck southern Mozambique before continuing across southern Africa. Combined with earlier flooding, it caused a humanitarian disaster.

properties.name: Cyclone Gafilo
properties.description: Cyclone Gafilo was the most intense tropical cyclone in the Southwest Indian Ocean since Cyclone Geralda. It struck northeastern Madagascar, killing over 360 people and leaving 214,000 homeless.

properties.name: Cyclone Gamede
properties.description: Cyclone Gamede set the world record for rainfall in 72 hours, with 3,929 mm recorded at Cratère Commerson on Réunion. It caused severe flooding and infrastructure damage.

properties.name: Cyclone Idai
properties.description: Cyclone Idai was one of the worst African cyclones on record. It devastated Beira, Mozambique, with extreme storm surge and winds, then caused catastrophic flooding in Zimbabwe and Malawi.

properties.name: Cyclone Kenneth
properties.description: Cyclone Kenneth was the strongest cyclone to make landfall in Mozambique on record. It struck just six weeks after Cyclone Idai, an unprecedented occurrence for the country.

properties.name: Cyclone Batsirai
properties.description: Cyclone Batsirai struck eastern Madagascar as an intense tropical cyclone, causing widespread devastation in the Vatovavy and Fitovinany regions. Over 100,000 people were displaced.

properties.name: Cyclone Freddy
properties.description: Cyclone Freddy was the longest-lived tropical cyclone ever recorded, lasting 36 days and crossing the Indian Ocean twice. It caused devastating floods and landslides in Malawi, killing over 1,400 people.

properties.name: Cyclone Chido
properties.description: Cyclone Chido was a devastating cyclone that struck Mayotte, a French overseas territory, in December 2024. It caused severe destruction on the island and then impacted northern Mozambique.

properties.name: Cyclone Tracy
properties.description: Cyclone Tracy devastated Darwin, Australia, on Christmas Day 1974, destroying 70% of the city's buildings. It led to the evacuation of most of the city's population and a complete rebuilding effort.

properties.name: Cyclone Ofa
properties.description: Cyclone Ofa caused widespread destruction across Samoa, Tonga, and Niue. It generated massive ocean swells that reached as far as New Zealand and disrupted communications across the region.

properties.name: Cyclone Heta
properties.description: Cyclone Heta struck Niue with devastating force, destroying the national hospital, museum, and much of the commercial district. Massive 50-meter waves battered the island's coastline.

properties.name: Cyclone Ingrid
properties.description: Cyclone Ingrid was a powerful Category 5 cyclone that struck remote parts of the Cape York Peninsula. It was one of only a handful of Category 5 cyclones to cross Cape York.

properties.name: Cyclone Larry
properties.description: Cyclone Larry made landfall near Innisfail, Queensland, causing $1.5 billion in damage. It devastated banana and sugar cane plantations across the Wet Tropics region.

properties.name: Cyclone Yasi
properties.description: Cyclone Yasi was one of the most powerful cyclones to hit Australia, making landfall near Mission Beach, Queensland. The 300 km wide core caused destruction across a huge area of North Queensland.

properties.name: Cyclone Pam
properties.description: Cyclone Pam was one of the strongest South Pacific cyclones on record, devastating Vanuatu. It caused widespread destruction in Port Vila and across the archipelago.

properties.name: Cyclone Winston
properties.description: Cyclone Winston was the strongest tropical cyclone to make landfall in the Southern Hemisphere. It struck Fiji with peak winds of 295 km/h, devastating Koro Island and the northern coast of Viti Levu.

properties.name: Cyclone Gita
properties.description: Cyclone Gita was the worst tropical cyclone to hit Tonga since 1982. It caused extensive damage to Tongatapu, including the destruction of the Parliament building in Nuku'alofa.

properties.name: Cyclone Harold
properties.description: Cyclone Harold rapidly intensified to Category 5 and struck Vanuatu's Espiritu Santo island with devastating force. It also impacted Solomon Islands, Fiji, and Tonga during the COVID-19 pandemic.

properties.name: Cyclone Seroja
properties.description: Cyclone Seroja caused devastating floods and landslides in Indonesia's East Nusa Tenggara province before making a rare landfall in Western Australia's midwest coast as a Category 3 cyclone.

properties.name: Cyclone Cody
properties.description: Cyclone Cody brought damaging winds and heavy rainfall to Fiji and Tonga in early 2022. It contributed to flooding across the archipelago in the lead-up to the Hunga eruption.

properties.name: Cyclone Gabrielle
properties.description: Cyclone Gabrielle was New Zealand's most damaging storm in decades, causing catastrophic flooding and landslides across the North Island. It declared a national state of emergency for only the third time.

properties.name: Cyclone Kevin
properties.description: Cyclone Kevin was a powerful Category 4 cyclone that struck southern Vanuatu, following closely behind the twin Cyclones Judy and Kevin sequence. It caused widespread damage in Tanna and Erromango.

properties.name: Cyclone Jasper
properties.description: Cyclone Jasper made landfall near Wujal Wujal in Far North Queensland and then stalled, producing extraordinary rainfall. Cairns and surrounding areas received record rainfall, causing severe flooding.

properties.name: Hurricane Pauline
properties.description: Hurricane Pauline struck Acapulco and the coast of Guerrero, causing catastrophic mudslides and flash floods. The devastation in impoverished hillside communities was immense.

properties.name: Hurricane Manuel
properties.description: Hurricane Manuel simultaneously impacted Mexico with Atlantic Hurricane Ingrid, causing catastrophic flooding. The dual-coast event killed over 160 people and devastated Acapulco and Sinaloa.

properties.name: Hurricane Odile
properties.description: Hurricane Odile was the strongest hurricane to hit the Baja California Peninsula in recorded history. It caused extensive damage in Cabo San Lucas and Los Cabos.

properties.name: Hurricane Patricia
properties.description: Hurricane Patricia was the strongest tropical cyclone ever recorded in the Western Hemisphere, with maximum sustained winds of 345 km/h. It made landfall in a sparsely populated area of Jalisco, limiting casualties.

properties.name: Hurricane Willa
properties.description: Hurricane Willa was a powerful Category 5 hurricane that struck Mexico's Sinaloa coast. It weakened to Category 3 at landfall but still caused significant damage to coastal communities.

properties.name: Hurricane Otis
properties.description: Hurricane Otis underwent unprecedented rapid intensification from a tropical storm to a Category 5 hurricane in just 12 hours before striking Acapulco. It caused $12 billion in damage.

properties.name: Hurricane Hilary
properties.description: Hurricane Hilary brought rare tropical moisture to Southern California, causing the first-ever tropical storm warning in the region. It caused significant flooding in Baja California and California's Inland Empire.

properties.name: Hurricane John
properties.description: Hurricane John made two separate landfalls in Mexico after an unusual track. It caused severe flooding in Acapulco and along the Guerrero coast, less than a year after Hurricane Otis devastated the area.

properties.name: Hurricane Madeline
properties.description: Hurricane Madeline struck the coast of Sinaloa and caused catastrophic flooding, killing over 400 people. It was one of the deadliest Eastern Pacific hurricanes of the 20th century.

properties.name: Hurricane Lane
properties.description: Hurricane Lane passed near the Hawaiian Islands, bringing record rainfall to the Big Island. Some locations on Hawaii Island received over 1,300 mm of rain, causing severe flooding.

properties.name: Hurricane Iniki
properties.description: Hurricane Iniki was the most powerful hurricane to strike Hawaii in recorded history, devastating the island of Kauai. It caused $3.1 billion in damage and destroyed 1,400 homes.

properties.name: Hurricane Emilia
properties.description: Hurricane Emilia was a powerful Category 4 hurricane that tracked westward across the Eastern Pacific. While remaining offshore, its outer bands brought rough surf and rip currents to the Mexican coast.

properties.name: Typhoon Olga
properties.description: Typhoon Olga struck the Philippines, causing devastating flooding and mudslides across Luzon. It was one of the deadliest typhoons in the Philippines during the 1970s.

properties.name: Typhoon Mireille
properties.description: Typhoon Mireille was one of the costliest natural disasters in Japanese history, causing $6 billion in insured losses. It struck western Japan with widespread wind damage.

properties.name: Typhoon Angela
properties.description: Typhoon Angela (Rosing) was the strongest typhoon to hit the Philippines in decades, with devastating winds across southern Luzon. It caused over 880 deaths and massive agricultural damage.

properties.name: Typhoon Usagi
properties.description: Typhoon Usagi was the most powerful typhoon in the world in 2013 at its peak. It struck the coast of Guangdong Province, China, causing significant damage and displacing hundreds of thousands.

properties.name: Typhoon Hagupit
properties.description: Typhoon Hagupit (Ruby) struck the Philippines near Dolores, Eastern Samar, just one year after the devastating Typhoon Haiyan. Pre-emptive evacuations of over 1 million people prevented a repeat catastrophe.

properties.name: Typhoon Haishen
properties.description: Typhoon Haishen struck Kyushu with powerful winds and storm surge before impacting the Korean Peninsula. It was the second major typhoon to hit the region within a week, following Typhoon Maysak.

properties.name: Cyclone Gonu
properties.description: Cyclone Gonu was the strongest tropical cyclone on record in the Arabian Sea. It struck Oman's northeastern coast, causing $4.4 billion in damage and then impacted southern Iran.

properties.name: Cyclone Chapala
properties.description: Cyclone Chapala was the strongest tropical cyclone to make landfall in Yemen on record. It brought several years' worth of rainfall to the arid coast in just two days.

properties.name: Cyclone Vardah
